h1Title: Dentigerous Cyst: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: A dentigerous cyst is the most common developmental odontogenic cyst, arising from fluid accumulation between the dental follicle and the crown of an unerupted tooth. It accounts for roughly 20-24% of all jaw cysts and occurs most frequently around impacted mandibular third molars, followed by maxillary canines and mandibular premolars. Most lesions are asymptomatic and discovered incidentally on routine dental radiographs as a well-defined radiolucent rim encircling the crown of an unerupted tooth. When large, dentigerous cysts expand cortical bone, displace adjacent teeth, and — rarely — transform into ameloblastoma or mucoepidermoid carcinoma. Surgical enucleation with tooth removal is curative in the majority of cases, with recurrence rates below 5%.

keyStats.prevalence: Dentigerous cysts represent 20-24% of all jaw cysts; estimated incidence 1.44 per 100 impacted teeth (Shear & Speight 2007)
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in the second and third decades; slight male predominance (male-to-female ratio approximately 1.6:1); mandibular third molars account for 50-70% of cases
keyStats.avgAge: Most frequently diagnosed between ages 10 and 30 years
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global prevalence unknown; considered the second most common odontogenic cyst after radicular (periapical) cyst

definition: A dentigerous cyst (ICD-10: K09.0), also called a follicular cyst, is a developmental odontogenic cyst that envelops the crown of an unerupted or partially erupted tooth by accumulating fluid between the reduced enamel epithelium and the tooth crown. The fluid buildup exerts osmotic and hydrostatic pressure that progressively expands the bony crypt. Pathologically, the cyst is lined by non-keratinising stratified squamous epithelium 2-4 cells thick, supported by fibrous connective tissue that is often inflamed. The lumen contains a proteinaceous, straw-coloured fluid rich in soluble proteins and cholesterol crystals in older lesions. Dentigerous cysts arise in the mandible in approximately 65% of cases and the maxilla in 35%, with a marked predilection for sites of tooth impaction — particularly the lower third molar, upper canine, and lower second premolar. Management is directed by oral and maxillofacial surgeons. Although the cyst is benign, large lesions carry a small risk of neoplastic transformation, most notably to ameloblastoma (estimated lifetime transformation rate under 1%), unicystic ameloblastoma, or rarely mucoepidermoid carcinoma, justifying histopathological examination of all excised tissue.

typesClassification.description: The follicle expands symmetrically around the tooth crown; the most common pattern. The tooth root is not involved and the lesion is usually unilocular on radiograph.
Fluid accumulates eccentrically on one side of the crown, often resulting from an obstruction of the follicular canal; more likely to be confused with a lateral periodontal cyst.
The cyst envelops the entire crown and part of the root, appearing to swallow the tooth on imaging; less common and may require more extensive surgery.
Periapical infection from a deciduous predecessor tooth stimulates proliferation of reduced enamel epithelium around a developing permanent tooth, producing a cyst with a thick inflamed wall.

primarySymptoms: No symptoms in most small lesions; incidental discovery on a dental panoramic radiograph as a well-defined, corticated, radiolucent rim of 3 mm or more around the crown of an unerupted tooth.
Painless, slow-growing jaw swelling causing visible facial asymmetry when the cyst expands beyond cortical bone, most often in the posterior mandible or upper canine region.
Failure of a permanent tooth to erupt at the expected age, particularly mandibular third molars, maxillary canines, or mandibular premolars.
Displacement and tipping of adjacent erupted teeth as the expanding cyst pushes them from their normal position.
Thinning and bowing of the lingual or buccal cortical plate detectable on bimanual palpation, sometimes with 'egg-shell crackling' or a fluctuant sensation.
Dull intermittent jaw ache or pressure sensation when secondary infection develops, occasionally accompanied by purulent discharge through a sinus tract.
Trismus or difficulty opening the mouth when a large mandibular cyst encroaches on the pterygomandibular or masseter space.
Paraesthesia or numbness of the lower lip and chin if the cyst abuts or displaces the inferior alveolar nerve canal.
Pathological fracture of the mandible at the angle or body in very large, long-standing cysts that have thinned the cortex to paper-width.
earlyWarningSigns: A retained deciduous tooth beyond age 14 paired with a missing permanent successor on panoramic X-ray — prompts assessment for a developing dentigerous cyst.
Any pericoronal radiolucency wider than 3 mm around an unerupted tooth crown on dental radiograph, the threshold used in most oral surgery guidelines.
Unexplained jaw swelling in an adolescent or young adult, even if painless, warrants panoramic radiography to exclude a cystic lesion.
emergencySigns: Acute cellulitis with fever, trismus, and rapid jaw swelling suggesting infected dentigerous cyst spreading to facial spaces — requires urgent hospital admission, IV antibiotics, and surgical drainage.
Sudden loss of lower-lip sensation in a patient with a known mandibular cyst, indicating acute nerve compression requiring immediate surgical decompression.
Pathological jaw fracture with malocclusion, crepitus, and inability to close the mouth — orthopantomogram and CT are needed urgently and stabilisation precedes definitive cyst surgery.

causes.cause: Fluid accumulation between reduced enamel epithelium and tooth crown
causes.description: After enamel formation is complete, the reduced enamel epithelium remains loosely adherent to the crown. Osmotic imbalance or minor trauma allows fluid to accumulate in this space, exerting pressure that progressively separates the epithelium from the crown and expands the bony crypt over months to years.

Any tooth that fails to erupt at its expected time remains enveloped by its follicle, prolonging the period during which cyst formation can occur. Impaction is caused by arch-length deficiency, ectopic tooth position, or ankylosis of a deciduous predecessor.

causes.cause: Periapical infection of a deciduous predecessor tooth
causes.description: Chronic infection at the root of a primary tooth can stimulate the reduced enamel epithelium of the developing permanent tooth beneath it, initiating inflammatory follicular cyst formation before the permanent tooth has erupted.
Some dentigerous cysts arise without an obvious stimulus; minor developmental irregularities in enamel organ regression or follicular blood supply are postulated but not proven.

Multiple dentigerous cysts should prompt investigation for Gorlin-Goltz syndrome (basal-cell nevus syndrome), mucopolysaccharidoses type IV, and cleidocranial dysplasia, in which follicular cysts form around multiple supernumerary or normal unerupted teeth.

riskFactors.description: Mandibular third-molar impaction occurs in 20-25% of Western populations; pericoronal radiolucency exceeding 3 mm around the crown is present in roughly 1-5% of impacted wisdom teeth and warrants close monitoring.
Palatal canine impaction occurs in about 2% of the population; dentigerous cysts are a recognised complication and a common reason for late surgical exposure.
Males have a 1.5- to 2-fold higher incidence, possibly related to greater rates of tooth impaction and delayed eruption.
Cyst formation coincides with the period of permanent-tooth development and eruption; lesions diagnosed after age 40 are less common and require more careful differentiation from other jaw cysts.

Patients with Gorlin-Goltz syndrome (PTCH1 mutation) develop multiple odontogenic cysts including dentigerous cysts alongside basal-cell naevi and skeletal anomalies, usually in the first two decades.
Untreated periapical infections in deciduous teeth increase the risk of inflammatory dentigerous cysts around their permanent successors; early dental care reduces this risk.

affectedDemographics: Peak incidence in the second and third decades of life; rare before age 5 and uncommon after age 40.
Male-to-female ratio approximately 1.6:1 in published oral surgery series (Shear & Speight 2007).
Mandibular third molars account for 50-70% of cases; maxillary canines and mandibular second premolars are the next most affected teeth.
Gorlin-Goltz syndrome patients present with multiple dentigerous-type cysts at a younger age (typically before age 20), often as an initial diagnostic finding.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with clinical suspicion raised by a missing tooth in the dental arch, jaw swelling, or an incidental radiolucent lesion on a dental panoramic radiograph. The classical radiographic appearance is a well-defined, corticated, unilocular radiolucency encircling the crown of an unerupted tooth, attached at the cemento-enamel junction. A follicular space wider than 3 mm is the widely cited threshold for designating a pericoronal radiolucency as abnormal, though some guidelines use 5 mm. CBCT (cone-beam computed tomography) delineates the three-dimensional extent, relationship to the inferior alveolar nerve, cortical perforation, and root displacement of adjacent teeth more precisely than conventional radiographs, and is recommended before surgery for any lesion larger than 2 cm or in close proximity to vital structures. MRI is rarely required but can distinguish cyst from soft-tissue tumour when CT findings are equivocal. Aspiration biopsy of the cyst lumen yields straw-coloured, low-viscosity fluid with a high soluble-protein content, which helps exclude the more viscous keratinous contents of an odontogenic keratocyst. Definitive diagnosis requires histopathological examination of the entire cyst wall — a non-keratinised stratified squamous epithelial lining 2-4 cells thick on fibrous stroma, without the corrugated parakeratinised lining that characterises an odontogenic keratocyst. All excised tissue should be submitted to oral pathology.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging; reveals pericoronal radiolucency, tooth position, and displacement of adjacent structures
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient bites on a localiser and stands while the machine rotates; takes under 20 seconds. Delivers a low radiation dose. Results reviewed immediately at chair-side.

diagnosticTests.purpose: 3-D delineation of cyst extent, cortical perforation, nerve proximity, and adjacent tooth root resorption
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Seated or standing scan lasting 10-20 seconds with slightly higher radiation than OPG. Specialist software reconstructs the jaw in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes; reviewed by the surgeon before planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiates fluid-filled cyst (straw-coloured, low-viscosity) from keratinous cyst or solid tumour; can be done chair-side under local anaesthesia
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anaesthetic is injected; a needle is passed through the overlying mucosa into the cyst. Any fluid drawn back is sent to pathology. Mild discomfort for a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used for large or radiographically atypical lesions where neoplastic transformation must be excluded before definitive treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small portion of the cyst wall is removed under local or general anaesthesia and sent to oral pathology. Results typically in 5-7 working days.

diagnosticTests.test: Histopathological examination of excised cyst wall
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis and excludes ameloblastoma, unicystic ameloblastoma, or odontogenic keratocyst lining
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The entire cyst wall is sectioned in the laboratory and read by an oral pathologist. The surgeon and patient receive the report within 1-2 weeks.

treatmentOverview: Treatment selection depends on cyst size, the tooth involved, patient age, and proximity to vital structures. For small cysts (under 2 cm) around a tooth of no functional value — most commonly an impacted mandibular third molar — enucleation and simultaneous tooth extraction is the standard approach. The cyst lining and tooth are removed under local anaesthesia as a day-case procedure, the bony cavity is curetted clean, and wound closure is primary. Recurrence after complete enucleation is below 5%. For large cysts (above 3 cm), particularly around a tooth that could eventually erupt (such as a displaced canine or premolar in a child), marsupialization — surgically creating an opening to allow the cyst to decompress into the oral cavity — is preferred as the first stage. Decompression shrinks the cyst over 6-18 months, preserves bone volume, reduces surgical risk to the inferior alveolar nerve, and often allows the tooth to erupt spontaneously or be guided orthodontically into the arch. Once the cyst has sufficiently reduced, secondary enucleation is performed if needed. Carnoy's solution application to the bony cavity is not routinely used for dentigerous cysts (unlike odontogenic keratocysts) because the recurrence rate with enucleation alone is low. All excised tissue should be sent for histopathological examination without exception, as ameloblastoma can arise from the cyst lining and is indistinguishable clinically. Postoperatively, panoramic radiography at 6 and 12 months confirms bony fill and absence of recurrence. Multiple dentigerous cysts should trigger genetic investigation for Gorlin-Goltz syndrome.

medicalTreatments.name: Surgical enucleation under local anaesthesia
medicalTreatments.description: The cyst lining is removed intact along with the associated unerupted tooth via an intra-oral incision. Performed as a day-case procedure; the bony cavity is curetted and primary closure achieved. Most appropriate for cysts under 2-3 cm in diameter.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Recurrence rate below 5% in published series; complete resolution of bony cavity confirmed on radiograph within 12-24 months

medicalTreatments.name: Marsupialization (decompression)
medicalTreatments.description: A window is cut in the cyst wall and sutured to the oral mucosa, creating a permanent opening that equalises pressure and allows progressive bone fill. A drain or iodoform gauze packing maintains patency. The patient irrigates the cavity daily.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cyst volume reduction of 50-80% within 6-12 months; often allows associated tooth to erupt or be orthodontically guided; used for lesions exceeding 3 cm or in proximity to critical structures

medicalTreatments.name: Decompression with delayed enucleation
medicalTreatments.description: Staged approach: marsupialization first reduces cyst size and nerve risk, followed by formal enucleation under general anaesthesia once the lesion has shrunk. Preserves the inferior alveolar nerve in the majority of cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Nerve preservation rate above 90% in mandibular third-molar cysts treated with staged decompression in retrospective cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Orthodontic-surgical collaboration for tooth salvage
medicalTreatments.description: After marsupialization, orthodontic traction is applied to draw a previously impacted tooth (canine, premolar) into its functional position in the arch once space has been created by cyst decompression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful tooth eruption or alignment achieved in 60-80% of adolescent patients managed with combined decompression and orthodontics

surgicalOptions.name: Enucleation with primary bone grafting
surgicalOptions.description: Large bony defects after cyst removal may be filled with autogenous bone, xenograft, or alloplastic material to accelerate osseous healing and maintain jaw contour.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Grafted cavities show complete or near-complete radiographic fill by 12-18 months in approximately 85% of cases

surgicalOptions.name: Mandibular reconstruction for pathological fracture
surgicalOptions.description: A fractured mandible resulting from a large dentigerous cyst may require open reduction and internal fixation with titanium plates and screws, combined with cyst enucleation. Reconstruction plates or free fibula flap are used for extensive defects.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Consolidation achieved in 90-95% of non-irradiated patients; free-flap reconstruction for large resections carries 90% flap success at experienced centres

alternativeTreatments.name: Platelet-rich fibrin (PRF) as cavity adjunct
alternativeTreatments.description: PRF membranes placed in the post-enucleation cavity are reported in small case series to accelerate bone fill by releasing growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β); evidence is limited to case series but adverse effects are minimal.

whySeeSpecialist: An oral and maxillofacial surgeon should evaluate any jaw cyst to determine the correct diagnosis, plan surgical access, and ensure that excised tissue is submitted to a qualified oral pathologist. General dentists can identify the lesion radiographically but are not trained to exclude neoplastic transformation or perform the staged decompression needed for large lesions.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a surgeon with a fellowship in oral and maxillofacial surgery who has experience managing odontogenic cysts and access to an oral pathology laboratory. For complex cases — large lesions near the inferior alveolar nerve, recurrent cysts, or multiple cysts suggesting a syndrome — a tertiary hospital-based unit is preferred.
hospitalCriteria: Access to CBCT imaging for surgical planning
In-house or affiliated oral pathology laboratory
Day-case surgical facility with local and general anaesthesia capability
Orthodontic team available for tooth-salvage cases
keyFacilities: Oral and maxillofacial surgery departments at dental schools or university hospitals
Tertiary maxillofacial units with reconstructive surgery capability
Community-based oral surgery practices for straightforward enucleation
preventionStrategies: Attend regular dental check-ups with panoramic radiography at recommended intervals to detect pericoronal radiolucencies before they enlarge.
Treat periapical infections of primary teeth promptly to prevent inflammatory stimulation of follicular epithelium around permanent successors.
Follow eruption timelines: missing permanent teeth by expected ages should trigger radiographic investigation, not watchful waiting.
For patients with Gorlin-Goltz syndrome or cleidocranial dysplasia, establish a dedicated annual OPG surveillance protocol from childhood.
lifestyleModifications: After marsupialization, irrigate the cavity with saline or prescribed chlorhexidine mouthwash twice daily to prevent secondary infection.
Maintain regular soft diet immediately post-operatively for one to two weeks; avoid hard, sharp foods that can displace the drainage packing.
Attend all post-operative appointments; radiographic follow-up at 6 and 12 months is necessary to confirm healing.
Avoid smoking during the healing period; smoking impairs wound healing and increases infection risk after jaw surgery.
Report any recurrent swelling, numbness, or pain in the operated area promptly — rare cases of recurrence or neoplastic transformation present this way.

recommended: Soft, easily chewed foods (yoghurt, scrambled eggs, mashed vegetables, soup) for the first 2 weeks post-operatively
High-protein intake (1.0-1.2 g/kg/day) to support tissue healing after oral surgery
Adequate vitamin C (75-90 mg daily) to support collagen synthesis and wound healing
Increased water intake to aid wound irrigation and systemic recovery
avoid: Hard, crunchy, or sharp foods that could dislodge wound sutures or cavity packing in the first 2-4 weeks
Carbonated drinks in the first week as they may impede blood-clot formation
Alcohol during antibiotic and analgesic courses
Tobacco in all forms throughout the surgical healing period

exerciseGuidelines: Rest for 48-72 hours after surgery. Light daily walking is encouraged from day 3. Strenuous exercise, contact sports, and activities that raise blood pressure significantly should be avoided for at least two weeks to minimise bleeding risk and swelling.
dailyManagement: Irrigate the marsupialization cavity daily using a syringe and saline as instructed by the surgeon.
Take prescribed analgesics on schedule for the first 48-72 hours rather than waiting for pain to peak.
Apply cold compresses to the cheek for the first 24 hours in 15-minute intervals to reduce swelling.
Attend panoramic radiograph reviews at 6 and 12 months post-operatively to confirm bone fill.
Maintain meticulous oral hygiene including tooth brushing up to but not in the wound area from day 2.
prognosis: Dentigerous cysts treated by complete enucleation carry an excellent prognosis, with recurrence rates below 5% in the published literature. Small to medium cysts healed by secondary bone fill within 12-24 months of enucleation, with near-normal radiographic density in the majority of patients. Marsupialization-first strategies for large cysts reduce the risk of nerve damage and pathological fracture during definitive surgery; combined decompression and late enucleation achieves nerve preservation in over 90% of mandibular cases. The main prognostic concern is the low but finite risk of neoplastic transformation: ameloblastoma has been reported in the lining of a small proportion of dentigerous cysts, with an estimated transformation rate below 1%. Histopathological review of all excised tissue is therefore mandatory. Patients with Gorlin-Goltz syndrome face a lifelong risk of new cyst formation and require surveillance. Overall, most otherwise-healthy patients with a single dentigerous cyst can expect complete resolution after a single surgical episode.
recoveryTimeline: Post-operative swelling and discomfort peak at 48-72 hours and resolve within one to two weeks. Sutures are removed at 7-10 days. For marsupialization, irrigation continues for 3-6 months. Radiographic evidence of complete bone fill appears at 12-24 months.
complications: Neoplastic transformation to ameloblastoma or unicystic ameloblastoma — uncommon (under 1%) but serious; mandates histopathological examination of all excised cyst wall.
Inferior alveolar nerve damage causing permanent lower-lip and chin numbness — risk is proportional to cyst size and nerve proximity; staged decompression reduces this risk significantly.
Pathological mandibular fracture in large, longstanding cysts that thin the cortex — prevent by early treatment before the cyst exceeds 3-4 cm.
Secondary infection producing acute osteomyelitis — presents as pain, fever, and purulent discharge; treated with IV antibiotics and surgical drainage.
Recurrence after incomplete enucleation — below 5% with thorough curettage; thorough histopathological sampling excludes re-classification as odontogenic keratocyst.
Damage to adjacent tooth roots or displacement of erupted teeth — reported in large cysts; carefully avoided with preoperative CBCT planning.

supportResources.name: American Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons (AAOMS)
supportResources.description: Patient education resources on jaw cysts, tooth impaction, and oral surgery procedures from the North American specialty body.

supportResources.name: British Association of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeons (BAOMS)
supportResources.description: Patient information leaflets on jaw cysts, surgical procedures, and post-operative care from the UK specialty association.

confusedWithConditions.name: Odontogenic Infection / Dental Abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: An odontogenic abscess presents with acute pain, swelling, and fever arising from an infected tooth root, whereas a dentigerous cyst is typically painless and found around an unerupted tooth crown. Radiographically, an abscess shows periapical lucency at the root apex, not the crown. Aspiration yields pus rather than straw-coloured cyst fluid.

confusedWithConditions.name: Impacted Wisdom Teeth (without cyst)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Impacted third molars normally have a pericoronal space of up to 3 mm; this becomes a dentigerous cyst only when the radiolucency exceeds 3-5 mm and shows a well-defined corticated border. A non-cystic impacted wisdom tooth does not have a discrete fluid-filled sac and resolves with extraction alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Odontogenic Keratocyst (Keratocystic Odontogenic Tumour)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Odontogenic keratocysts (OKC) also appear as pericoronal or inter-radicular jaw radiolucencies but are distinguished by a corrugated parakeratinised lining on histopathology, a higher recurrence rate (25-60%), a tendency to grow along the medullary cavity with little cortical expansion, and association with Gorlin-Goltz syndrome. Aspiration yields keratin-rich 'cheesy' material rather than watery cyst fluid.

relatedConditions.name: Odontogenic Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Dentigerous cysts can become secondarily infected, converting to an odontogenic abscess requiring immediate surgical drainage and antibiotics.

relatedConditions.name: Impacted Wisdom Teeth
relatedConditions.relevance: The commonest tooth associated with dentigerous cysts is the impacted mandibular third molar; the two conditions are frequently managed together.

relatedConditions.name: Oral Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: A rare but clinically important complication of a longstanding dentigerous cyst is neoplastic transformation, most commonly to ameloblastoma or mucoepidermoid carcinoma, making histopathological review essential.

faqs.question: What is a dentigerous cyst?
faqs.answer: A dentigerous cyst is a fluid-filled sac that forms around the crown of an unerupted tooth when fluid accumulates between the tooth and its surrounding tissue. It is the most common developmental jaw cyst, accounting for about 20-24% of all jaw cysts, and is most often found around impacted wisdom teeth.

faqs.question: What causes a dentigerous cyst?
faqs.answer: The cyst forms when fluid builds up between the reduced enamel epithelium and an unerupted tooth crown, gradually expanding the bony follicular sac. Tooth impaction, periapical infection of a primary tooth, and developmental variations in enamel organ regression are the main triggers. Certain syndromes like Gorlin-Goltz cause multiple cysts.

faqs.question: Is a dentigerous cyst painful?
faqs.answer: Most dentigerous cysts are painless and discovered by chance on dental X-rays. Pain develops when the cyst becomes infected or grows large enough to press on nerves. A dull ache, lip numbness, or jaw swelling may develop in larger lesions.

faqs.question: How is a dentigerous cyst diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with a dental panoramic radiograph showing a corticated radiolucent rim wider than 3-5 mm around an unerupted tooth crown. CBCT scanning maps the exact size and nerve proximity. Definitive diagnosis requires histopathological examination of the excised cyst wall.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for a dentigerous cyst?
faqs.answer: Small cysts are treated by surgical enucleation — removing the cyst lining and tooth together — with recurrence rates below 5%. Large cysts are first decompressed by marsupialization, which shrinks the cyst over months before final removal, protecting the nearby nerve.

faqs.question: Can a dentigerous cyst go away on its own?
faqs.answer: No. Dentigerous cysts do not resolve without treatment. They enlarge slowly over time, risk damaging adjacent teeth and nerves, and carry a small risk of becoming cancerous. Early surgical removal is the recommended approach.

faqs.question: Can a dentigerous cyst become cancerous?
faqs.answer: Yes, but rarely. The estimated neoplastic transformation rate is below 1%. A dentigerous cyst can give rise to ameloblastoma or, even less commonly, mucoepidermoid carcinoma. This is why all excised cyst tissue must be examined by an oral pathologist.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after dentigerous cyst removal?
faqs.answer: Swelling and discomfort peak at 48-72 hours and settle within two weeks. The bony cavity fills in over 12-24 months on radiograph. For staged marsupialization, daily irrigation continues for 3-6 months before the cavity closes.

faqs.question: What is marsupialization of a dentigerous cyst?
faqs.answer: Marsupialization is a surgical procedure that creates an opening from the cyst into the mouth, equalising pressure and allowing the cyst to shrink gradually. It preserves bone and nerves and is preferred for large cysts, particularly in children where tooth eruption is still possible.

faqs.question: Does a dentigerous cyst require general anaesthesia?
faqs.answer: Small to medium cysts can usually be removed under local anaesthesia as an outpatient procedure. Larger lesions, anxious patients, or cases requiring bone grafting may be performed under IV sedation or general anaesthesia in a day-surgery unit.

faqs.question: What are the signs of an infected dentigerous cyst?
faqs.answer: An infected cyst produces pain, facial swelling, fever, trismus (difficulty opening the mouth), and sometimes a purulent discharge. These signs require urgent hospital assessment and IV antibiotics before definitive surgical treatment.

faqs.question: Can a dentigerous cyst cause tooth loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Large cysts can resorb the roots of adjacent erupted teeth, displacing and loosening them over time. Early removal of the cyst is the best way to preserve neighbouring teeth. The tooth associated with the cyst itself is usually extracted during surgery.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence rate for dentigerous cysts?
faqs.answer: Recurrence after complete enucleation is below 5% in published series. This is much lower than for odontogenic keratocysts, which recur in 25-60% of cases. Incomplete removal or misdiagnosis of the cyst type are the main reasons for recurrence.

faqs.question: Are dentigerous cysts more common in men or women?
faqs.answer: Dentigerous cysts are about 1.6 times more common in men than women, likely because men have higher rates of tooth impaction. The cysts are most frequently found in the second and third decades of life, coinciding with wisdom tooth development.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a dentigerous cyst and an odontogenic keratocyst?
faqs.answer: Both appear as jaw radiolucencies but differ in lining: a dentigerous cyst has a thin, non-keratinised epithelium with low recurrence, while an odontogenic keratocyst has a corrugated parakeratinised lining and a much higher recurrence rate of 25-60%. Aspiration fluid is straw-coloured in dentigerous cysts but keratinous and creamy in odontogenic keratocysts.

simpleName: Dentigerous cyst (follicular cyst, jaw cyst around unerupted tooth)
metaDescription: Dentigerous cyst: fluid sac around an unerupted tooth crown causing jaw swelling. Causes, diagnosis, enucleation vs marsupialization treatment reviewed here.

Neville BW, Damm DD, Allen CM, Chi AC. 2015. Oral and Maxillofacial Pathology. 4th ed. Elsevier, St Louis.
